children.name: Pharomachrus
children.description: The resplendent quetzals — arguably the most visually stunning birds of the Neotropics, with iridescent green plumage, crimson bellies, and extraordinarily long upper tail coverts that cascade like a serpent's tail. The males' elongated tail feathers were sacred to Mesoamerican civilisations, used as currency and ceremony by Aztec and Maya nobility. The word quetzal comes from the Nahuatl quetzalli, meaning 'precious feather'.

children.name: Pharomachrus mocinno
children.description: The most famous trogon, found in highland cloud forests from southern Mexico to western Panama. The male is a living jewel, with iridescent green head and body, a crimson breast, and tail streamers up to one metre long. It was sacred to the Maya and Aztec civilisations and is the national bird of Guatemala, lending its name to the country's currency.

children.name: Pharomachrus auriceps
children.description: Found in the humid montane forests of the Andes from Colombia and Venezuela south to Bolivia. As its name suggests, the male has a shimmering golden-bronze head and bronzy-green upperparts with a red belly. It replaces the Resplendent Quetzal in the Andean cloud forests.

children.name: Pharomachrus antisianus
children.description: A poorly known quetzal of the Andean foothills from Venezuela to Bolivia. It is distinguished from the Golden-headed Quetzal by its more prominent crest and green (not bronzy) head. Its ecology remains little studied due to its remote cloud-forest habitat.

children.name: Trogon
children.description: The largest and most widespread trogon genus, comprising over 20 species across the Neotropics. Trogons are typically green or blue above with a contrasting red, orange, or yellow belly, and share a characteristic habit of sitting motionless on a perch before sallying out to catch insects or fruit. They nest in cavities excavated in rotting wood or termite mounds.

children.name: Trogon elegans
children.description: The most northerly-distributed trogon, found from the south-western United States (Arizona, New Mexico, Texas) through Mexico to Costa Rica. The male has a green head and chest, blue-green back, and coral-red belly and undertail. It inhabits dry canyons and riparian woodlands, where its slow, repeated call is a characteristic sound.

children.name: Trogon collaris
children.description: A widespread and variable trogon of humid lowland and montane forests from southern Mexico through the Amazon basin. The male has a green head and breast with a distinctive white band separating the breast from the red belly. Females are brown with a similar collar pattern.

children.name: Trogon personatus
children.description: A montane species found in cloud forests from Colombia and Venezuela south to Bolivia. The male has a green head and upper breast, a distinct white eye-ring, and a bright red belly. It is most easily distinguished from the similar Collared Trogon by its more restricted white chest band and the absence of barring on the undertail.

children.name: Priotelus
children.description: A small genus of trogons endemic to the Caribbean, with two species restricted to Cuba and Haiti. Their plumage is greener and less iridescent than mainland trogons, with distinctive white-tipped tail feathers.

children.name: Priotelus temnurus
children.description: The national bird of Cuba, endemic to the island where it is known as the tocororo. It has a green back, grey breast, red belly, and striking white-tipped tail feathers. Its three-note call is said to sound like 'tocoro-ro', and its blue-white-red plumage mirrors the colours of the Cuban flag.

children.name: Apaloderma
children.description: The only trogon genus found outside the Americas, with three species distributed across sub-Saharan Africa. African trogons are more arboreal and forest-dependent than their Neotropical counterparts, and are among the most elusive and poorly studied birds on the continent.

children.name: Apaloderma narina
children.description: The most widespread African trogon, found from West Africa through the Congo Basin to East Africa and south to South Africa. The male has a brilliant metallic green head and upper breast, crimson-red underparts, and a striking bare patch of blue-green skin around the eye. Named after Narina, the lover of the French ornithologist FranÃ§ois Le Vaillant, it is a shy inhabitant of dense forest canopy.
